coredata.dc:title: Blue luminescent graphene quantum dot conjugated cysteamine functionalized-gold nanoparticles (GQD-AuNPs) for sensing hazardous dye Erythrosine B
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Farmani, Maryam Rahmati
Peyman, Hossein
Roshanfekr, Hamideh
coredata.dc:description: Erythrosine B (ErB) is a xanthenes approved dye that widely used in various fields such as foods, drugs and cosmetics. This work intended to analyze the interaction of this dye on synthesized graphene quantum dot conjugated cysteamine functionalized gold nanoparticles. Pyrolysis of citric acid was applied to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesis. These are zero dimensional materials promising considerable applications because of their extraordinary physicochemical properties. In this work, to improve its applications conjugates of GQDs and gold nanoparticles (GQD-AuNPs) are synthesized and characterized with FESEM, TEM, EDX and FTIR techniques. Interaction of ErB with synthesized conjugates was investigated using fluorescence technique and the results demonstrate that ErB can quench fluorescence of GQD-AuNPs conjugates, considerably. At last, we used of this good interaction for construction sensor for detection of ErB in the concentration range of 1.2 nM to 50 nM and the obtained LOD was 0.03 nM (S/N = 3) with correlation coefficient of (99%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Erythrosine B
Cysteamine functionalized AuNPs
Graphene quantum dot (GQD)
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction of ErB dye with GQD-AuNPs
ErB Detection by the GQDs-AuNPs conjugate
Interaction of ErB dye with nanoparticles
Interaction of ErB dye and GQDs
The interaction of ErB dye and cysteamine-capped AuNPs
Interaction of ErB dye with GQD-AuNPs conjugates
Fluorescence based GQD-AuNPs sensor for ErB determination
Comparison of quenching effect of ErB on AuNPs, CQD and GQDs-AuNPs conjugate and study of quenching mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV spectra GQD and AuNP, inset: UV spectra of GQD-AuNP.
FESEM images and energy dispersive spectra of A) AuNPs, B) GQD-AuNPs conjugates C) TEM images of GQDs.
FT-IR spectra of A) CA, B) GQD, C) GQD-AuNPs.
Reduced fluorescence intensity of GQD in the presence of ErB, inset: reduced fluorescence of AuNPs after interaction with ErB.
Fluorescence intensity of GQD-AuNPs in different concentration of ErB, inset: calibration curve of ErB detection by fluorescence reduce of GQD-AuNPs.
Stern-Volmer plots for quenching of GQD-AuNPs, GQD and AuNPs by ErB.
Schematic synthesis of: A) GQDs, B) cysteamine-capped AuNPs, C) fabrication of GQD-AuNP conjugates.

head.ce:title: Blue luminescent graphene quantum dot conjugated cysteamine functionalized-gold nanoparticles (GQD-AuNPs) for sensing hazardous dye Erythrosine B

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ilam Branch, Islamic Azad University, Ilam, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of chemistry, Ilam branch, Islamic Azad University, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Erythrosine B (ErB) is a xanthenes approved dye that widely used in various fields such as foods, drugs and cosmetics. This work intended to analyze the interaction of this dye on synthesized graphene quantum dot conjugated cysteamine functionalized gold nanoparticles. Pyrolysis of citric acid was applied to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesis. These are zero dimensional materials promising considerable applications because of their extraordinary physicochemical properties. In this work, to improve its applications conjugates of GQDs and gold nanoparticles (GQD-AuNPs) are synthesized and characterized with FESEM, TEM, EDX and FTIR techniques. Interaction of ErB with synthesized conjugates was investigated using fluorescence technique and the results demonstrate that ErB can quench fluorescence of GQD-AuNPs conjugates, considerably. At last, we used of this good interaction for construction sensor for detection of ErB in the concentration range of 1.2 nM to 50 nM and the obtained LOD was 0.03 nM (S/N = 3) with correlation coefficient of (99%).

ce:section.ce:para: Erythrosine B (ErB) is an organiodine compound and a fluorone derivative. It is a melone-pink synthetic food colorant and it is used for dyeing variety of materials, such as wool, silk and nylon, drugs and cosmetics [1,2].

The consumption of artificial colorants should be under the ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake) limit. Due to high consumption of colorants, people may be exposed to high amounts of chemical compounds, especially children because of their weak body compared to adults.

ErB mainly consists of disodium 2-(2,4,5,7-tetraiodo-3-oxidooxoxanthen-9-y1) benzoate monohydrate and tends to decompose in the wastewater, which leads to health problems [3]. Although, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved the ErB, but it has an influence on children behavior, and thyroid function due to the high iodine content [4,5].

Inhibition of drug-metabolizing enzymes, potent inhibition of two protein–protein interactions involving the tumor necrosis factor (TNF) superfamily were proven by in vitro studies [6]. Also some studies indicate that at low concentrations ErB can induce DNA damage in the gastrointestinal organs [7].

To take into consideration above points, the toxicity and carcinogenic nature of the erythrosine attracted universal consideration for determination and trying to removal of erythrosine from wastewater [8]. One of proposed materials for determination of Erythrosine B is photocatalysts.

Semiconductor materials are used as photocatalysts. Heterogeneous photocatalysis is an emerging technique for environmental problems. These photocatalysts decompose the organic pollutants, and then pollutant molecules are adsorbed on the surface of the catalysts. In this step chemical bonds are broken and formed, and ultimately small organic molecules are released as products [9].

So, advanced oxidation processes (AOP) have been widely studied in degradation reactions of pollutants [10–12]. Today, heterogeneous photocatalysis apply AOP to mineralize of organic compounds. In this process, hydroxyl radicals HO° are generated when the photocatalyst is illuminated in the presence of water and air [13–15]. These ultra-reactive species associated with oxygen are able to achieve a complete mineralization of organic pollutants into carbon dioxide, water and other non-toxic products [16,17].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), are widely used new luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials because of their optical and electronic properties. GQDs are graphene sheets with lateral size smaller than 100 nm in single, double and multiple layers, and diameters spanning the range 3–20 nm mainly. These materials possess special properties including low toxicity, high biocompatibility, high fluorescent activity, robust chemical inertness and excellent photostability by effect of quantum confinement and edge (armchair or zigzag) effect. These properties confer GQDs a variety of potential uses in photovoltaic devices, bioimaging instruments, sensors and biosensors, among others [18,19].

So far, GQDs have been produced by using top-down or bottom-up methods. The former includes electron beam lithography, chemical vapor deposition (CVD), sonication, electrochemical preparation and etc. [20–29]. Most top-down methods use carbon black, graphene oxide (GO).

The bottom-up methods involve solution chemistry [30]; carbonization of organic precursors such as glucose [31], citric acid [32] or HBC (hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene) [33], or fragmentation of C60 [34]. The bottom-up method is often preferred, because it is relatively easy to perform, it produces high quantum yields, and it offers precise control over the morphology and size of the quantum dots. However, these bottom-up methods are time consuming and include the need to optimize the hydrothermal treatment, temperature control and object selection [31].

Like other carbon-based materials, GQDs exhibit largely size-dependent photoluminescence (PL), which has been ascribed mainly to quantum confinement, composition, structure and shape. GQD photoluminescence typically ranges from blue to green or less commonly, yellow to red, with smaller GQDs having longer PL emission wavelengths than larger ones [35]. The presence of oxygen-containing (carbonyl, epoxy, hydroxyl, and carboxyl) functional groups at the edge of GQDs makes them very readily soluble in water and in most polar organic solvents [23].

Graphene quantum dots have scarcely been used in analytical chemistry. The few, recent exceptions involve the determinations of glucose [36], free chlorine in drinking water [37], Cd2+ [38], Fe3+ [39], pyrocatechol [40], immunoglobulin G [41] and specific DNA sequences [8,42].

Previously, the amazing properties of metal nanoparticle such as Au nanoparticles have become a remarkable area of research since materials in the nanometer dimension show size-dependent optical, electronic, and chemical properties. So these materials are potential in developing nanosensors and applicable nanodevices in biology and optoelectronics [43–46].

Recently, it has been reported that the AuNPs supported on carbonaceous materials exhibited higher catalytic activity, selectivity, and better environmental suitability than those attached onto other substrates, especially the commonly used metal oxides [45,47–49]. It was discovered that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) alone and the nanoarchitectures assembled with GQDs could dramatically improve the activity and stability of the catalysts, due to the synergetic effect raised by GQDs that possess excellent electron conductivity and good chemical/physical stabilities [50,51].

The aims of this work were: (1) to synthesize the GQDs by pyrolyzing CA; (2) to synthesize of AuNPs functionalized with cysteamine and its conjugation with luminescent GQDs; (3) to research the interaction of ErB with GQDs-AuNPs conjugates using the quenching of the emitted fluorescence; (4) optimizing the experiment conditions; and (5) finally determination of ErB by synthesized GQDs-AuNPs.

All chemical reagents were analytical-grade and used without further purification. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) (HAuCl4·3H2O), cysteamine hydrochloride (HSCH2CH2NH2·HCl), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), sodium choloride (NaCl), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dehydrate (H2NaPO4.·2H2O), Erythrosin B (ErB) (C20H8I4O5), and ethanol were all purchased from Merck Co. Citric acid (CA) and ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich and Alfa Aesar, respectively. All glassware was rinsed with chromic acid solution and double-distilled water at least three times. All the solutions were prepared using deionized water.
The fluorescence studies were carried out in 0.01 M phosphate buffer solution containing 0.1 M NaCl (pH 7.0, PBS).

To synthesize GQDs, a memmert oven and to homogenize GQDs nanoparticles and GQD/AuNP conjugates, an ultrasonic Elmasonic P60H were applied. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on Perkin Elmer, Lambda 25 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy were obtained by a Perkin Elmer LS 45 Fluorescence spectrometer and a Perkin Elmer Spectrum GX FTIR system, respectively.

In order to study the single and conjugated nanoparticles, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), F ESEM MIRA3 was applied. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were made on a Zeiss-EM10C-80KV instrument.

GQDs were obtained via pyrolysis of CA according to previous studies (Scheme 1A) [32,52,53]. First, 2.0 g CA was put in a 10 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C in the oven for 30 min. The CA melted within 5 min and changed into colorless then pale yellow and after 30 min, turned into an orange viscose liquid that indicates the formation of GQDs. After that the obtained liquid was added into 100 mL 10 mg mL− 1 NaOH drop by drop under strong stirring, then it was put on the stirrer for 10 min and the pH was adjusted to 8.0 by adding NaOH and HCl solutions, thus an aqueous solution of blue-luminescent GQDs was obtained. In order to dry GQDs solution, 30 mL ethanol was added into it using rotary and vacuum pump, after 24 h, the powder of GQDs was obtained which showed high purification.

Cysteamine-capped AuNPs were synthesized by chemical reduction of gold chloride solution using the following method: 400 μL 213 mM cysteamine hydrochloride, was added into 40 mL 1.42 mM HAuCl4 solution under strong stirring for 20 min in dark, then 10 μL cold NaBH4 solution was added into it using micropipette and vigorously stirred for 10 min in dark followed by gentle stirring for 30 min. For more stabilization the obtained cysteamine-capped AuNPs solution was stored in the dark condition at 4 °C for 24 h (Scheme 1B) [54–56].

To synthesize GQD-AuNP conjugates, 0.038 g EDC was solved in 1 mL PBS buffer (pH 7.4) followed by 5 min sonication, and then added to 5 mL of cysteamine-capped AuNPs solution. Next, 40 mg GQD was solved in 5 mL PBS buffer (pH 7.4) and sonicated 5 min then added to the solution obtained in the previous step and shook for 1 h, followed by centrifugation (10,000 rpm) for 10 min. Finally, the GQD-AuNP conjugates were obtained (Scheme 1C) [56,57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of ErB dye with GQD-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the interactions, three different solution containing 3 mL 10− 6 M ErB with (1) 500 μL 5 mg mL− 1 GQD, (2) 500 μL cysteamine-capped AuNPs, and (3) 500 μL GQD-AuNP conjugates were prepared, separately, then, the fluorescence studies were carried out in the excitation wavelength of ErB dye and GQD-AuNPs.

Seven solutions of 10− 6 M ErB dye, with different pH (pHs 3–9), were prepared in PBS buffer, then the fluorescence spectra were obtained in the excitation wavelength of ErB dye (475 nm) [58]. 50 μL GQD-AuNP conjugates solution was added into ErB dye solutions separately and fluorescence studies were carried out in different excitation wavelength: λex(dye) = 475 nm, λex( AuNP) = 521 nm [59–61], and λex(GQD) = 380 nm [52,59] immediately and after 24 h, then the intensities of fluorescence emission were compared.

500 μL GQD-AuNP conjugates solution was added into two separated 3 mL 10− 6 M dye solutions (in PBS buffer with pH 7), then one of the them was stored in light and the other in dark and every 1 h, the fluorescence spectra were recorded (λ ex = 380 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ErB Detection by the GQDs-AuNPs conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: At first, the solution of GQDs-AuNPs conjugate was prepared in PBS buffer (pH 8; 0.1 M) (solution A). Then several samples contain constant amount of A and different concentration (0.02–200 μM) of ErB were prepared in optimum condition (total volume: 2 mL). The fluorescence spectra were recorded immediately and a calibration curve for ErB was drawn.

Pyrolysis is a simple bottom-up method to obtain photoluminescent GQDs by low carbonization of CA and spreading the carbonized products in alkaline solutions [32]. During pyrolysis, adjacent CA molecules interact with each other and will be dehydrated and form GQDs with functional groups such as –OH, –CH2, –COOH, and –COOR located at the edge and act as a good functional layer on the surface. This layer facilitates the uniform grouping of the sp2 clusters in the GQDs structure; these clusters are partially isolated in sp3 matrix and increase the fluorescence of GQDs [53]. The diameter of GQDs increases by increasing the heating time that leads into decreased quantum fluorescence yield, so during pyrolysis the optimizing the heating time is very important [53].

By irradiating GQDs with UV-radiation at 368 nm, they emit strong blue radiation at 456 nm (Fig. S1). Although the fluorescence mechanism of GQDs is not clear, but there are some reasons for their high photoluminescence activity: 1) the effect of the zigzag edge [22,62,63], 2) the effect of small size [64–66], and 3) the effect of the surface passivation [67,68]. The other logical reason is that GQDs contains a lot of small and isolated sp2 clusters which are homogenized in size [53].

As it mentioned before, the obtained GQDs have many functional groups containing oxygen that are the source of luminescence activity, also, –OH and –COOH groups make GQDs stable and soluble in water. The photoluminescence emission of GQDs is independent from excitation which indicates that the size and surface structure of sp2 clusters in GQDs should be uniform [32].

The maximum UV–Vis absorption of GQDs is at 368 nm, while CA, as a precursor of GQDs, has not absorption in this wavelength (inset; Fig. S1), and it has absorption below 250 nm, indicating the size uniformity of sp2 clusters in GQDs. These results show that most of the π-π bonds have been formed during pyrolysis, so there is lots of π-network in GQDs; it may due to dehydration of CA molecules during heating [53]. The wavelength of the absorption peak of GQDs is almost the same as that of its excitation peak, indicating that there is a little non-emitting absorption in GQDs (As shown in Fig. S1) [32], therefore by comparing the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of GQDs and CA, it can be concluded that GQDs are obtained.

The AuNPs were synthesized by chemical reduction of gold chloride solution using NaBH4 as a reducing agent and cysteamine as a coating factor for nanoparticles stability. Cysteamine contains sulfur and positively charged amine functional group, so it can interact with AuNPs via sulfur end and the positively charged amine functional groups are free and make electrostatic repulsion among AuNPs leading into stability of AuNPs solution. Cysteamine-capped AuNPs containing amine groups are dark red in color. The maximum absorption peak of AuNPs is at 526 nm, implying that the size of particles is in nanoscale and these results are in accordance with the results of SEM studies (Fig. 1 ).

In addition to color change, UV–Vis spectra results can verify the functionalization of nanoparticles (Fig. 1) [56]. In comparison with GQDs and AuNPs absorption peaks (λmax = 456 and 526 respectively), there is a red-shift and broadened in GQD-AuNPs absorption peak, indicating that particles are in cluster structure and it may due to neutralization of positive charges on the cysteamine-capped AuNPs by binding to negative charges of GQDs [56].

According to the FESEM studies of cysteamine-capped AuNPs, the obtained AuNPs are spherical with 11–34 nm in size which the average size is 23 nm (Fig. 2A). The FESEM images were recorded to investigate GQD-AuNP conjugates, as it shown in Fig. 2B, the isolated conjugated nanoparticles show no significant changes in size, implying the small size of GQDs nanoparticles compared with AuNPs [56].
TEM technique is often useful to use together with SEM. The TEM image indicates that the size of GQDs is mostly distributed in the range of 6–11 nm (85%) (Fig. 2C).

The spectrum of energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) of AuNPs presents the peaks of Au, S, C, and O verifying the obtained cysteamine-capped AuNPs (Fig. 2A). The EDX of GQD-AuNPs conjugates shows C and O and Au peaks, indicating the successful conjugation (Fig. 2B) [56].

The FT-IR (Fig. 3A) spectra of CA and GQDs were recorded to investigate any spectral changes in functional groups during the synthesis. The stretching vibrations of functional groups in CA are: OH (344 cm− 1), CO (1641 cm− 1), CH (2850–3000 cm− 1), COO– (1363 cm− 1), and COH (1212 cm− 1) and in stretching vibrations of functional groups in GQDs are: OH (3470 cm− 1), CH (2892 cm− 1), CO (1641 cm− 1),COH (1276 cm− 1), and there are two intense peaks (1582 and 1398 cm− 1), implying the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of COO– group (Fig. 3B). The results indicate that the synthesis of GQDs containing –CH2, –OH, and –COOH groups is successful [53].

The absorption of –COOH and –OH groups in GQDs indicates that the GQDs sheets synthesized via carbonization of CA contain many hydrophilic groups such as –COOH, also, the recorded stretching vibrations of CH (2892 cm− 1) and CH lower than 1350 cm− 1 shows the low carbonization of GQDs, implying that the synthetized GQDs contain very low amount of non-pyrolyzed CA [32].

By comparing the FT-IR spectra of CA and GQDs, it can be concluded that the GQDs are obtained from CA because the absorption intensity of CO group relating to CA is significantly reduced in comparison with that of GQDs absorption spectra [32]. As shown in Fig. 3A and B, in GQDs absorption spectra, there is no new absorption peak, implying that during the pyrolysis the type of functional groups has not undergone any changes but the structure of CA and number of those significantly have changed [53].

In Fig. 3C, the bonding of GQDs to cysteamine is presented, the stretching vibrations of CN and NH of amine groups, OH, CO, and CO groups and bending vibration of NH of amine groups are 1361, 3067, 3662, 1727, 1303, and 1483 cm− 1 respectively. The peaks of NH and CN groups are the result of interaction of negatively charged –COO– group related to GQDs with positively charged amine groups of cysteamine-capped AuNPs, also OH and CO peaks show that the groups containing oxygen in GQDs have not interacted during synthesis [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of ErB dye with nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the interaction of ErB dye with GQD-AuNP conjugates, the interaction of ErB dye with GQDs and AuNPs was studied separately then compared with the interaction of ErB dye and GQD-AuNP conjugates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of ErB dye and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: When the ErB dye was added to GQDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs reduced in the presence of ErB dye during the reaction (Fig. 4 ). The GQDs contain vast π network including –OH and –COOH functional groups at the edges. There are aromatic rings with double bonds in ErB dye, therefore ErB-GQD complex can be obtained via strong reaction of π-π bonds of these functional groups in GQDs and ErB. Adding ErB molecules and formation of ErB-GQD complex lead into the fluorescence quenching of GQDs. The fluorescence emission of GQDs at 380 nm can be inhibited by a suitable energy receiver, ErB dye. So the fluorescence of GQDs can be quenched [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction of ErB dye and cysteamine-capped AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The reduced fluorescence of AuNPs was observed during the interaction with ErB dye (Inset; Fig. 4) due to two reasons: 1) the chemical affinity to interactions depends on the degree of hardness and softness of atoms. Au is a soft metal with high affinity to interaction with non-metal iodine atom in ErB dye and iodine is known as a fluorescence quencher molecule, therefore by increasing ErB dye concentration, the interaction of Au and I would be increased that leads to decreased fluorescence emission(quenching) until all AuNPs are saturated with ErB dye. 2) The cysteamine-capped AuNPs contain positively charged amine groups that can electrostatically interact with the anionic ErB dye, which is negatively charged, therefore, the ErB dye will attach to the surface of cysteamine-capped AuNPs strongly via electrostatic interaction [69–71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of ErB dye with GQD-AuNPs conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: According to the results, ErB dye has high affinity to interact with GQDs and AuNPs, therefore it can interact with the GQD-AuNPs conjugates with high affinity, too (as it shown in Fig. S2).

As well as the mentioned mechanisms involved in the interaction of ErB dye with nanoparticles, there are other possible mechanisms for interaction of ErB dye with GQD-AuNPs conjugates: ErB dye molecules can be excited by UV photoexcitation and reach to excited singlet state then they undergo intersystem crossing to their triplet state.

GQDs also can be photoexcited that leads into the formation of electron hole pairs; therefore, GQDs can participate in more redox reactions. The generated hole in GQDs can get an electron from the triplet ErB dye resulting formation of cation that reacts with OH– and leads into OH° radical as real oxidizing agents.

The GQDs electrons can be dissolved in oxygen molecules and then they can be removed leading to the formation of superoxide anion radicals (O2°−) that react with water molecules more and produce more OH° radicals. The semiconductors GQDs undergo this process for more reactions. Oxidation of colorless gaseous products is a result of OH° radical reaction with ErB dye molecules. Participation of OH° radicals in the reaction was verified by using the electron acceptor like hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the reaction mixture [72].

Therefore, ErB dye molecules attach to the nanoparticles leading to increased fluorescence emission. Removing the ErB dye molecules by photocatalytic activity of nanoparticles resulted into decreased activity of electron hole pairs in semiconductor GQDs leading to decrease the intensity of fluorescence emission (Fig. S2) [73].
The results indicate that the photocatalytic activity of GQD-AuNP on ErB dye strongly depends on the pH of solution. The reactions were investigated in pH range of 3–9 (Fig. S3).

The interaction will increase with increasing the pH to 7, but in the pH higher than 7, the fluorescence intensity of GQD-AuNPs conjugates will decrease, implying that the reaction between ErB dye and GQD-AuNPs conjugates is decreased.

Therefore, pH of solution has a significant effect on the surface charge of GQD-AuNPs conjugates and it can be concluded that the surface charge of GQD-AuNP conjugates has an impact on the absorption and interaction with ErB dye molecules [74–76].

According to the mentioned mechanism, –OH– group is necessary in photocatalytic reaction. Decreasing in hydroxy anion concentration led into the acidic pH that resulted into the reduced photocatalytic reaction. In the pH higher than 7, more –OH– groups can be produced that makes the surface of GQD-AuNPs negatively charged. The repulsion between the negative charges on the surface of GQD-AuNPs and anion groups in the dye, thus the interaction of dye molecules with the surface of GQD-AuNPs can be more difficult that leads into decreased interaction and fluorescence intensity.

The interaction of ErB dye and GQD-AuNPs conjugates took place mostly in the first 3 h then due to decreased in the amount of free ErB dye, the interaction will significantly reduce (as it shown in Fig. S2). Also, comparing the interaction in light and dark indicates that the light accelerates the interaction that results into difference in the intensity of fluorescence peaks (Fig. S4).
Our deep study in interaction between ErB dye and GQDs, AuNPs, GQD-AuNPs conjugate, separately, shows the GQD-AuNPs conjugate can be good candidate for trace determination of ErB dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence based GQD-AuNPs sensor for ErB determination
ce:section.ce:para: The GQD-AuNPs conjugate shows good stability, water solubility and strong interaction with ErB dye, so it was directly used for determination of ErB dye. The mechanism of interaction and fluorescence quenching of GQD-AuNPs conjugate was illustrated above. Fig. 5 shows the quenching of GQD-AuNPs fluorescence by ErB addition, moreover it was observed a good linear relationship between the quenched fluorescence intensity and concentration of added dye. Thus, the calibration curve was created by plotting the fluorescence reduction of GQD-AuNPs versus the added concentration of ErB dye (1 mg mL− 1). The observed quenching was linear in concentration range 1.2 nM to 50 nM and the obtained LOD was 0.03 nM (S/N = 3) with correlation coefficient of (99%) (inset; Fig. 5).

In compare with the previous work on the determination of Erythrosine B, with a detection limit of 0.4 nM and a linear range of 0.4–450 nM, our proposed method has a better detection limit and better detection range. In addition, the method presented by our group is much simpler and less costly than capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection (CE-LIF) method that was applied for ErB determination, recently [77].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of quenching effect of ErB on AuNPs, CQD and GQDs-AuNPs conjugate and study of quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The application of fluorescence spectroscopy to investigate the interaction between different molecules has been verified to be valuable due to its high sensitivity and reproducibility. The decline of the quantum yield of fluorescence of a fluorophore is known as fluorescence quenching that is induced by a variety of molecular interactions, such as excited-state reactions, molecular rearrangements, ground-state complex formation, collisional quenching, and energy transfer [78].

The different mechanisms of fluorescence quenching are usually categorized as either static quenching or dynamic quenching. Static and dynamic quenching can be differed from each other with their different dependence on temperature and viscosity [79]. Since higher temperatures result in larger diffusion coefficients, the bimolecular quenching constants are expected to increase along with temperature increase. In contrast, increased temperature is likely to result in decreased stability of complexes, and thus lower values of the static quenching constants.

The fluorescence quenching of AuNPs, CQD and GQDs-AuNPs conjugate by ErB were compared by calculating the well-known Stern–Volmer equation:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of fluorophore in the absence and presence of quencher Q, respectively, KSV is the Stern–Volmer constant (M− 1) and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher.

Stern–Volmer plots for the fluorescence quenching are shown in Fig. 6 . As can be seen, all plots are linear with the slopes of 3.09 × 104, 1.81 × 104 and 1.46 × 104 M− 1 for GQDs-AuNPs conjugate, CQD and AuNPs, respectively. The higher Ksv for conjugate of GQDs-AuNPs is indicative of higher binding affinity of ErB to the conjugate.

Also, for determining the quenching mechanism, we calculated the Stern-Volmer constants for the GQDs-AuNPs conjugate-ErB system at three different temperatures (25, 35 and 45 °C). The result illustrates that the Stern–Volmer quenching constant KSV is inversely correlated to temperature, which indicates that the quenching mechanism is static quenching rather than by dynamic quenching.

Due to the toxicity and carcinogenic nature of the erythrosine, in the present work, we studied its interactions with a fluorescent probe to design a new optical sensor for its determination. At first GQDs emitting blue fluorescence were obtained via pyrolysis of CA. Then the AuNPs were synthesized by chemical reduction of gold chloride solution using NaBH4 as a reducing agent and cysteamine as a coating factor for nanoparticles stability. Positive charges on cysteamine-capped AuNPs attract negative charges of GQDs. The interaction of ErB dye with conjugated GQDs was investigated using fluorescence technique. The results show ErB can quench fluorescence of GQDs-AuNPs conjugates. The good performance can be attributed to the synergic cooperation between GQDs and AuNPs. At last, we used of this good interaction for construction an ErB sensor. This sensor performs better than some existing competing methods for detection of ErB.
H. Peyman: Presenting research ideas, designing and managing different stages of research, doing software calculations, and writing and editing articles.
M. Rahmati Farmani: Performing practical research steps and gathering information for the paper.
H. Roshanfekr: Supervising the implementation of the research process, modifying the necessary methods, and collaboration in writing and editing article text.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117960.
